current_week_words.casual_usage: りんごたべる？(Ringo taberu?) = 'Want to eat an apple?' flows naturally between friends. Pair it with juice: りんごジュース (ringo jūsu) is one of the most common drink orders in Japan.
current_week_words.formal_usage: The word doesn't change form in formal settings — just adjust what surrounds it. りんごをいただきました (ringo wo itadakimashita) = 'I received an apple' is perfectly polite when thanking someone for a gift.

current_week_words.casual_usage: Between friends, りょこういく？ (Ryokō iku? = 'Going on a trip?') is a natural opener, especially around holidays. You'll also catch らいしゅうりょこうしようよ ('Let's travel next week!') which flows easily once you know the word.
current_week_words.formal_usage: The word itself doesn't change — only the surrounding grammar shifts up. よいりょこうを (Yoi ryokō wo) is a warm, standard farewell meaning 'Have a good trip,' and it fits everywhere from a coworker send-off to a hotel check-out.
